one_line_takeaway: For wide NTK-regime ReLU networks doing Sobolev-space regression, early stopping is provably minimax-optimal against bounded-radius input perturbations, while training to the interpolation limit provably destroys adversarial robustness (even when it doesn't hurt standard L2 accuracy), and the one tested post-hoc mitigation (randomized α-smoothing) only fixes this in low dimensions.
key_finding: All three theory pieces are empirically corroborated by U-shaped adversarial-risk-vs-training-time curves across all three datasets: risk falls to a minimum near an early-stopping point (consistent with Theorem 4.2's minimax-optimal rate) and then rises again as training continues toward the interpolation limit (consistent with Theorems 5.1/5.3's divergence bound), with the wide ReLU network's spiky, noise-interpolating function surface visibly explaining the loss of local smoothness (Figure 2). The proposed mitigation is only a partial fix: α-trimmed randomized smoothing keeps adversarial risk stable even at ~10^6 training steps on the 1D synthetic data, but performs poorly (does not prevent the rise in adversarial risk) on the high-dimensional synthetic and real-world Diabetes data -- the authors explicitly flag this as a limitation of the smoothing approach that 'warrants further investigation,' not a solved defense.
core_contribution: The paper gives a theoretical study of adversarial robustness for wide (NTK-regime) ReLU neural networks doing nonparametric regression on Sobolev-class target functions. Three results are the paper's own, built on but distinct from cited prior work: (1) it derives matching minimax lower and upper bounds (Theorem 3.3: rate ≍ r^{2min(1,s)} + n^{-2s/(2s+d)}) for adversarial risk over a Sobolev ball H^s(L), using a projected/truncated kernel ridge regression estimator, plus a Lepski's-method-based adaptive estimator (Theorem 3.5) that nearly matches this rate without knowing the smoothness index s -- the authors note in Remark 3.1 that the rate form is 'similar to Peng and Yang [2025]' but for a different (Sobolev vs. Holder) function class, so the novelty is the Sobolev/RKHS setting, not the rate itself; (2) it proves that wide NTK neural networks trained by gradient flow with early stopping at t* = n^{(d+1)/(2s+d)} achieve this same minimax-optimal adversarial rate (Theorem 4.2), using a new RKHS-Sobolev equivalence (Lemma 4.1, s = (d+1)/2, which leans on a norm-equivalence result from Chen et al. [2024]) combined with an early-stopping optimality argument building on Li et al. [2024]'s L2 (non-adversarial) result -- the adversarial version is the paper's own; (3) it shows overfitting destroys adversarial robustness: the minimum-norm interpolant (t→∞ limit of gradient flow) has adversarial risk lower-bounded by D*log(n*r_n^d) whenever n*r_n^d→∞ (Theorems 5.1 and 5.3, the latter generalizing a lemma from Lai et al. [2023] on the smallest NTK-matrix eigenvalue to arbitrary dimension/depth), and separately proves (Theorem 5.5) that even the 'spiky' ridgeless kernel-interpolation estimator of Haas et al. [2023] -- which is L2-consistent ('benign overfitting') -- has diverging adversarial risk despite achieving standard consistency; the spiky-kernel construction (Proposition 5.4) itself is adapted from Haas et al., but the adversarial-divergence proof on top of it is the paper's contribution.
threat_model: A test-time evasion adversary with a fixed perturbation budget: for a test input X, the adversary may choose any point x' in the closed Euclidean ball B(X, r) ⊆ [0,1]^d and the model is scored on its worst-case squared error over that ball (Definition 2.1, adversarial risk R_A). This is a bounded-norm, white-box-strength (worst-case over the ball, no query/gradient-access constraints modeled) evasion threat model applied to a scalar regression output, not a classification decision boundary, and not a model-extraction, poisoning, or backdoor threat model. No real attacker, attack algorithm, or deployed system is instantiated; the adversary is purely an analytic worst-case perturbation operator used to define a risk functional that the paper bounds.
limitations: Explicitly stated by the authors: (1) results rely on the NTK (infinite-width, 'lazy training') approximation and do not cover finite-width networks or the feature-learning regime where the tangent kernel evolves during training; (2) the analysis fixes the input dimension d and does not address the high-dimensional regime d→∞ as n→∞; (3) adaptivity to the adversarial radius r is proven impossible when the smoothness s ≤ d/2 (Theorem 3.4), a genuine negative/impossibility result within the paper's own framework; (4) the randomized α-smoothing mitigation tested in Section 6.3 fails to control adversarial risk in higher dimensions and on real data, an open problem the paper does not resolve. Additional limitations evident from the text but not necessarily foregrounded by the authors: the adversary is an idealized worst-case Euclidean-ball perturbation on regression inputs, not validated against any concrete attack algorithm (e.g., PGD) or any classification-task threat model; there is no comparison to competing certified-defense baselines (e.g., IBP) despite citing them; and the empirical section uses small sample sizes (n up to 400, or 200 for Diabetes) and a single architecture (one hidden layer, m=100,000 width), so the simulations are illustrative of the theory rather than a robustness benchmark.

datasets_used: 1D synthetic regression: x ~ Unif(0,1), f*(x) = sin(2πx), additive Gaussian noise σ=0.3
Diabetes dataset (real-world): d=10 standardized physiological/blood-serum predictors, targets projected onto unit sphere S^{d-1}, source https://www4.stat.ncsu.edu/~boos/var.select/diabetes.tab.txt
High-dimensional synthetic regression: d=5, x ~ Unif([0,1]^5), f*(x) = sin(2π × mean(x)), additive Gaussian noise σ=0.3
quantitative_results: Theorem 3.3: minimax adversarial risk over Sobolev ball H^s(L) ≍ r^{2min(1,s)} + n^{-2s/(2s+d)}
Theorem 3.5 (Lepski adaptive estimator): risk ≲ (r^{2min(1,s)} + n^{-2s/(2s+d)}) × log^5(n)
Lemma 4.1: RKHS of ReLU NTK on [0,1]^d is norm-equivalent to Sobolev space H^s with smoothness index s = (d+1)/2
Theorem 4.2: wide network early-stopped at t* = n^{(d+1)/(2s+d)} achieves E[R_A] ≲ n^{-2s/(2s+d)} + r^{2min(1,s)}, matching the minimax lower bound
Theorem 5.1/5.3: minimum-norm interpolant adversarial risk R_A(f̂_∞, f*) ≥ D log(n r_n^d) whenever n r_n^d → ∞ (D depends on noise level C, dimension d, σ)
Theorem 5.5: ridgeless spiky-kernel interpolator (adapted from Haas et al. 2023) has R_A ≳ log(n r_n^d) for training time t > n^{6/(d+1)}, despite being L2-consistent

Section 6 simulations: adversarial radii r ∈ {0, 0.02, 0.05}; sample sizes n ∈ {50,100,200,400} (n ≤ 200 for Diabetes); wide ReLU net width m = 100,000, n=10, early stop at epoch 2500 (Figure 2); α-trimming smoothing with Gaussian std 0.1 and truncation rates α ∈ {0.35, 0.49}
baselines_compared: No external competitor-method benchmark is run; comparisons are internal to the paper's own estimators: early-stopped NTK/NN estimator vs. minimum-norm interpolant (t→∞) vs. ridgeless spiky-kernel interpolant of Haas et al. [2023] (adapted, not re-benchmarked as a defense), and un-smoothed vs. α-trimmed randomized-smoothing (adapted from Rekavandi et al. [2024]) variants of the NTK estimator

Certified-defense methods cited in Related Work (Randomized Smoothing, IBP/Gowal et al. 2018) and empirical defenses (PGD adversarial training, TRADES) are discussed as context but are not implemented or numerically compared against in this paper
